html:
Dentium is a better stock now than the stale “Korean China implant exporter” label suggests, but the old easy thesis was wrong in one important way: this is not a net-cash FCF monster today. It is a margin-recovery, share-count-shrink, China-volume-reset story trading at about 6 to 7x forward earnings while a very large buyback is mechanically lifting per-share economics.

At KRW 49,700, the stock has a roughly KRW 430 billion market value using the post-cancellation share count. Q1 2026 was the first genuinely interesting quarter in two years: revenue was still weak at KRW 71.4 billion, down 7% year over year, but operating profit rose 65% to KRW 15.9 billion and net income rose to KRW 17.1 billion. The key was a 76.7% gross margin, helped by lower-cost titanium inventory, consumables internalization, manufacturing efficiency, and FX. This is not yet a clean demand recovery, but it proves the cost structure has more earnings torque than the market was modeling.

The market is still focused on six straight quarters of revenue decline and China VBP uncertainty. That is fair, but incomplete. China is still 45% of Q1 revenue, so Dentium is not fully diversified. Yet the China problem has shifted from “pricing cliff” to “delayed procurement and destocking.” The next VBP round, now more likely in 2H26, can pressure ASPs but should also release deferred volumes. Straumann’s 2025 commentary shows the same industry pattern: China weak from delayed VBP and distributor destocking, while ex-China APAC, challenger brands, digital workflow, and value implants remain healthy.

The real variant view is that 2026 earnings can recover even before revenue looks exciting. Brokers now estimate roughly KRW 66 to 67 billion of 2026 net income and KRW 7,100 to 7,300 EPS before full benefit from the new buyback. Dentium already cancelled roughly 2.44 million treasury shares, cutting issued shares from 11.07 million to 8.62 million, and then announced a KRW 60 billion buyback trust through March 2027. Kiwoom noted the company had bought about 130,000 shares by May 12, leaving roughly 1.07 million more shares to purchase. If those are retired, the effective share base can fall another low-teens percentage. That alone can move normalized EPS toward KRW 8,000 to 9,000 without heroic operating assumptions.

This matters because the stock is being valued on stale 2025 earnings, which were distorted by weak China, inventory, and impairment. On 2026 earnings, Dentium trades near 7x P/E and around 5x EV/EBITDA. On a post-buyback 2027 setup, with revenue recovering toward KRW 420 to 440 billion, OP margin in the low-to-mid 20s, and net income near KRW 75 to 85 billion, the stock is closer to 5x earnings. That is too low for a global top-tier value implant manufacturer with 60% plus gross margins, high clinical credibility, and a large addressable market.

The activist angle is not cosmetic. Align Partners pushed on treasury share cancellation, board oversight, related-party transactions, Vietnam investments, and the fuel-cell distraction. It lost the main board fight but won a director compensation cap vote and kept public pressure alive. This turns governance from a permanent discount into a live catalyst. The company has already changed behavior by accelerating treasury cancellation and launching the buyback. If management stops treating cash flow as captive capital for unrelated fuel-cell ambitions and instead keeps buying stock below intrinsic value, the multiple should rerate.

The main risk is that Q1’s margin is temporarily flattered by low-cost inventory, while cash flow remains poor because inventory and capex absorb earnings. Mirae forecasts only modest 2026 to 2027 FCF despite much higher net income. That keeps this from being a clean self-liquidating value stock. The other risk is China VBP 2.0: if volume does not follow pricing, Dentium’s earnings recovery gets pushed out. A third risk is governance. The fuel-cell business and related-party questions are real, and the stock deserves a lower multiple until capital allocation is cleaner.

Still, the asymmetry is attractive. The market is pricing Dentium like a structurally impaired China cyclical, while the next 12 months could show three simultaneous upgrades: China volume normalization, sustained mid-to-high 60s gross margin, and a shrinking share count. That is enough to make a 7x forward P/E look wrong.

Bear target: KRW 35,000, down 30%. China VBP disappoints, margins fade back toward 18% operating margin, buyback execution slows, and the market values Dentium at 5x depressed EPS.

Base target: KRW 75,000, up 51%. 2026 EPS lands near KRW 8,000 after buybacks, China stabilizes in 2H26, Asia and Europe recover, and the stock rerates to 9 to 10x earnings.

Bull target: KRW 125,000, up 152%. VBP restarts China volume, Europe/Russia normalizes, Vietnam and Thailand keep growing, the buyback is fully retired, EPS power moves above KRW 10,000, and the market values Dentium as a global value-implant compounder at 12x earnings.
